answer:
In *Saqaliya*, Iqbal personifies Sicily as the "grave of Hejazi civilization," lamenting the lost vitality of Islam's early maritime conquerors who renewed the world and shattered superstition with their "thunderous" Takbir.[^1] The poem's aesthetic dwells on Sicily's enduring beauty amid ruins—"the honor of the sea," its "vistas" and "rocky shores" a poignant contrast to desolation—while politically evoking a once-world-shaking ummah reduced to silenced echoes, bequeathing its mourning to Iqbal to carry "as a gift to Hindustan."[^2]

This elegy expands in *Bilad-e-Islamiya*'s panoramic survey of Islamic cities—Delhi's "sacred ground" bloodied by forebears; Baghdad as Hijazi culture's "pride"; Cordoba, whose flame birthed Europe's civilization despite its extinguishing; Constantinople as the ummah's "eternal symbol"; and Medina as ultimate "homeland" and "sanctuary."[^3] Aesthetically, these "abodes" are shrines of light and garden-paradises, their dust rivaling Iram's; politically, they trace Islam's arc from sovereigns who made "the world's own order turn" to scattered assemblies, yet their spiritual essence endures, binding the ummah beyond geography (even privileging India as foundation).[^4]

Culminating at Delhi's Mughal tombs in *Goristan-e-Shahi*, Iqbal walks a "desolate and vast" royal graveyard under a "sullied" moon, where "suns of glory" lie amid universal transience—Egypt, Babylon, Persia, Greece, Rome, and now Muslims, a "cloud of March, that rose and rained, and did not last."[^5] The aesthetic revels in nature's defiant beauty (cypress, nightingale, rosebuds) piercing death's silence, mirroring the tombs' "glory"; politically, it indicts greed and frailty but insists grief harbors renewal—"in this cloud... bolts of lightning strain," able to "make the desert dust a rose."[^6]

The thread weaves a political-aesthetic jeremiad: Islam's civilizations—Sicily's conquests, cities' radiance, Mughal majesty—once aesthetically transfixed and politically remade the world, but empire's hubris invites inevitable graves. Yet ruins' eloquent silence demands Muslim mourning not as defeatism, but as aesthetic-spiritual catalyst: pain's "pearls" and latent thunder revive the ummah, urging Hindustan's heirs to resurrect Hijazi fire from Delhi's dust.[^7]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 21 (*Saqaliya*), [translation](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/).

[^2]: *Saqaliya*, ¶¶ [p-08760e](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-08760e), [p-379aeb](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-379aeb)–[p-a060f8](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-a060f8), [p-665fb2](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-665fb2).

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 2 (*Bilad-e-Islamiya*), [translation](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/).

[^4]: *Bilad-e-Islamiya*, ¶¶ [p-876603](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-876603)–[p-4ecfd3](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-4ecfd3), [p-c959b9](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-c959b9)–[p-9bfa99](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-9bfa99), [p-8c21df](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-8c21df)–[p-2686fa](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-2686fa), [p-c6a6c2](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-c6a6c2)–[p-f92993](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-f92993).

[^5]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 9 (*Goristan-e-Shahi*), [translation](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/09-goristan-e-shahi/translation/).

[^6]: *Goristan-e-Shahi*, ¶¶ [p-5921aa](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/09-goristan-e-shahi/translation/#p-5921aa)–[p-2e4cf2](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/09-goristan-e-shahi/translation/#p-2e4cf2), [p-6e0f0c](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/09-goristan-e-shahi/translation/#p-6e0f0c), [p-fad54d](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/09-goristan-e-shahi/translation/#p-fad54d)–[p-1b40c0](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/09-goristan-e-shahi/translation/#p-1b40c0), [p-56b241](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/09-goristan-e-shahi/translation/#p-56b241)–[p-2e5187](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/09-goristan-e-shahi/translation/#p-2e5187).

[^7]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 21 [translation](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/); *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 2 [translation](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/), ch. 9 [translation](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/09-goristan-e-shahi/translation/).
